pmos

Product Development Processes — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Skills & Processes. A product development process is the team's operating system — how work gets discovered, decided, built, and shipped — and almost every modern one is a different bet on the four preferences in the Agile Manifesto and its twelve principles. Scrum is the dominant framework (sprints, three accountabilities, five events, and the load-bearing admission that it is "purposefully incomplete"); Kanban drops the sprint for continuous flow governed by WIP limits; the two differ mainly on cadence, change-tolerance, and where authority sits, with complexity and planning horizon driving the choice. Shape Up is the clearest counter-proposal — shape, bet, build with appetite replacing estimates. Orthogonal to all of them is the discovery question: dual-track agile and continuous discovery run "what to build" alongside "how to build it" in one team, not two. The rituals (planning, standup, review, retro) are the feedback machinery; story points and the #NoEstimates debate are genuinely unsettled. At scale the Spotify model — squads, tribes, chapters, guilds — was famously never fully implemented even at Spotify, and SAFe answers the same coordination problem with structure instead of culture. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 17 verified sources.

What a "process" actually is — the team's operating system

A product development process is the operating system a team runs on: the standing answer to who decides what gets built, how that work is broken down, what rhythm it ships on, and how the team learns between releases. It is not the same as a methodology brand. "We do Scrum" names a framework; the actual process is the lived behavior — what happens in your planning meeting, whether your standup is a status report or a coordination huddle, how a stakeholder request becomes committed work. The brand is the label on the box; the process is what the team does on Tuesday.

Two clarifications save a lot of confusion. First, almost every framework in this primer is agile in lineage — they are competing implementations of one underlying philosophy, not unrelated systems. The Agile Manifesto set four value preferences, and each framework is a different bet on how to honor them in practice. Second, process is a means, not an identity. The Scrum Guide is explicit that its framework "is purposefully incomplete, only defining the parts required to implement Scrum theory" — which means a process is always a partial skeleton you wrap your own engineering and discovery practices around. A team that treats the skeleton as the whole body gets ceremony without outcomes.

The useful lens for a senior PM is to read any process as a set of trade-offs along a few axes — fixed-time versus fixed-scope, batch versus flow, autonomy versus alignment, prescription versus emptiness — and ask which trade-offs fit this team, this product, and this stage. The rest of this primer maps the canonical answers and where their advocates disagree.

The agile turn — the four values and twelve principles everything inherits

Modern product process begins with a reaction. In 2001 seventeen practitioners, frustrated with heavyweight sequential development, wrote the Manifesto for Agile Software Development. Its four values are stated as preferences: "Individuals and interactions over processes and tools; Working software over comprehensive documentation; Customer collaboration over contract negotiation; Responding to change over following a plan." The most-forgotten sentence is the qualifier underneath them — "while there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more." The right-hand items are not garbage to be thrown away; they are subordinated. Teams that read the Manifesto as "no documentation, no plans" are misreading it.

The twelve principles are the testable layer beneath the values: "Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software," "Welcome changing requirements, even late in development," and "Working software is the primary measure of progress." Principle ten — "Simplicity—the art of maximizing the amount of work not done—is essential" — is the one most processes quietly violate while claiming the badge. A team can adopt every Scrum ceremony and still fail most of these principles; the principles, not the rituals, are what "agile" was supposed to mean.

This matters for reading every framework that follows. Scrum, Kanban, Shape Up, dual-track, and the scaling frameworks are all downstream of these four preferences — each one a different engineering of the same intent. When two frameworks disagree (sprints versus flow, estimates versus appetite), they are usually disagreeing about how best to deliver value early and respond to change, not about whether to. Holding the principles in view keeps process debates from collapsing into tribalism.

Further reading: amazon.com — Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

Scrum — the dominant framework and its deliberate emptiness

Scrum is the default operating system for most product teams, and the Scrum Guide (Schwaber and Sutherland) defines it tersely: "Scrum is a lightweight framework that helps people, teams and organizations generate value through adaptive solutions for complex problems." It rests on three empirical pillars — transparency, inspection, and adaptation — which is the engine: make the work visible, inspect it frequently against a goal, adapt when you detect drift. The machinery that produces those loops is three accountabilities (Product Owner, who owns value and the backlog; Scrum Master, who owns the team's effectiveness with the framework; Developers, who build the increment) and five events (the Sprint as a container, plus Sprint Planning, the Daily Scrum, Sprint Review, and the Retrospective), with Sprints fixed at "one month or less."

The phrase that most experienced PMs underweight is "purposefully incomplete." Scrum deliberately says nothing about how you write code, how you do discovery, how you test, or how you estimate. That emptiness is a feature — it makes Scrum portable across domains — but it is also the source of most "Scrum doesn't work for us" complaints. The framework is a thin process membrane; if the team has weak engineering practices or no discovery habit, Scrum will faithfully sprint them into building the wrong thing efficiently. It coordinates work; it does not supply judgment.

The common failure mode worth naming is "mechanical Scrum" or what practitioners call zombie Scrum: the events happen on the calendar, but transparency, inspection, and adaptation don't. A retrospective that surfaces the same issues every fortnight without change is the canonical symptom — the ritual is intact, the empirical loop it exists to drive is dead. Scrum done well is the three pillars made real; Scrum done badly is the five meetings made mandatory.

Further reading: amazon.com — Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

Kanban — continuous flow and limiting work in progress

Kanban descends from the Toyota production system's signal cards and adapts that idea to knowledge work through two governing moves. Per the Kanban guide, "The Kanban method emphasizes visualizing the workflow and limiting work in progress (WIP)," and "Kanban centers on continuous delivery and encourages teams to optimize their workflow at every opportunity." There is no sprint. Work moves across a board (backlog → in progress → review → done) as a pull system: a team member draws the next item only when capacity frees up, rather than committing a batch of work at the start of a fixed iteration.

The load-bearing mechanism — and the one teams skip — is the WIP limit. Capping the number of items allowed in each column is what converts a board from a to-do list into a flow-control system. When the "in progress" column is full, you cannot start new work; you have to help finish what's there. This is counter-intuitive for teams that equate busyness with productivity, but limiting WIP is precisely what shortens lead time: less work in flight means less context-switching, fewer half-done items aging on the board, and faster cycle time per item. Kanban's core metrics — lead time, cycle time, throughput — fall directly out of treating the board as a queue to be optimized rather than a status display.

Where Scrum changes the team's behavior in discrete sprint-sized steps with a planning ritual at each boundary, Kanban changes continuously and uses the WIP limit, not a time-box, as the forcing function. That makes Kanban a natural fit for work with unpredictable arrival and stable priorities — support, platform, ops, incident-heavy teams — where committing a fixed sprint scope two weeks out would be fiction. It asks less ceremony of the team, but it demands more discipline about flow, because there is no sprint boundary to hide a stalled item behind.

Scrum vs. Kanban — and the Scrumban middle

The honest version of this comparison is not "which is better" but "which trade-offs fit." BrightWork draws the operational lines cleanly: "Scrum focuses on sprints that are a set period of time whereas Kanban cadence is focused on consistent and continuous workflow"; on change, "Kanban works best for teams with stable priorities that are unlikely to change over time. Scrum allows for changing priorities from sprint to sprint"; and on authority, "The Scrum Master acts as the chief problem solver, unlike Kanban where every team member shares responsibility and leadership." Three axes, then: cadence (batch vs. flow), change-tolerance (sprint-stable vs. continuously re-orderable), and where coordination authority sits.

The selection signals scale with organizational shape. AgileInsider sizes the choice on complexity: "The larger the complexity of deliverables in your organization, the larger its size...you should start veering toward Scrum," while "The smaller the complexity of deliverables, the smaller the size...you should keep hanging onto Kanban." Its readable proxies — org structure, team structure, number of products in the stack, and planning horizon — push quarterly-or-longer horizons and multi-product portfolios toward Scrum's planning ritual, and simpler, shorter-horizon, continuously-arriving work toward Kanban's flow.

In practice many teams land on Scrumban — keeping Scrum's cadence and review/retro loops while adopting Kanban's WIP limits and pull-based board inside the sprint. It is not a formal framework so much as the recognition that the two are not mutually exclusive: a sprint can contain a flow-managed board. The senior-PM point is that none of these are doctrines to be obeyed; they are dials. The teams that struggle are usually the ones that adopted a brand wholesale instead of choosing the cadence, change-tolerance, and authority model their actual work needs.

Further reading: amazon.com — Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

Shape Up — the Basecamp alternative that rejects sprints

Shape Up, from Basecamp's Ryan Singer, is the most coherent mainstream argument that agile need not mean sprints at all. Its keystone is the replacement of estimation with appetite, defined in the Shape Up book as "the amount of time we want to spend on a project, as opposed to an estimate." The reframe inverts the planning question from "how long will this take?" to "how much time is this worth?" — so scope becomes the variable that flexes to fit a fixed time budget, rather than the schedule flexing to fit a fixed scope. This is the inverse of the classic estimation trap, where optimistic estimates quietly stretch into missed deadlines.

The mechanism has three moves, which ProductPlan summarizes as giving teams "language and specific techniques to address the risks and unknowns at each stage of product development": shape (define a solution at the right altitude — concrete enough to be buildable, abstract enough to leave room for the team's judgment), bet (at a betting table during a two-week cooldown, stakeholders choose which shaped pitches earn a cycle — there is no continuously groomed backlog and no sprint-commitment ceremony), and build (one team gets six uninterrupted weeks). The circuit breaker is the disciplinary teeth: work that fails to ship within its cycle is cancelled rather than extended, which makes the deadline real from day one instead of a renegotiable target.

Shape Up's trade-offs are real and worth flagging rather than evangelizing. It assumes small, senior, trusted teams and a product where six weeks is a meaningful unit; it deliberately sacrifices the fine-grained predictability and backlog transparency that stakeholders in larger orgs often demand. It is contested whether it scales beyond Basecamp-sized teams — its advocates argue the principles (fixed time, variable scope, shaped work, betting over backlog) port even when the literal six-week cycle does not. Read it less as a turnkey replacement for Scrum and more as a sharp critique of two Scrum habits: the ever-growing backlog and the treatment of estimates as commitments.

Discovery and delivery — the dual-track reframing

Every framework above is mostly a delivery process — machinery for building and shipping. None of them, on their own, answers whether you are building the right thing. That is the gap dual-track agile addresses. Per LogRocket, it "integrates discovery efforts (determining what to build) with delivery efforts (building) while fostering collaboration across product, engineering, and design." Jeff Patton and Marty Cagan coined "dual-track scrum"/"dual-track agile" around 2012, and the framing is now table stakes in product thinking — but it is also one of the most-misread ideas in the field.

The misreading is structural, and LogRocket names it directly: the idea that "there are two separate teams for discovery and delivery or separate people with different responsibilities. This was never the intention." The two tracks are two kinds of work and two kinds of thinking done by the same cross-functional team in parallel — not a discovery team that hands a spec to a delivery team, and not a discovery phase that finishes before delivery begins. When orgs implement dual-track as a handoff, they recreate exactly the sequential waterfall it was meant to dissolve, with a "discovery" label on the front. The whole point is that discovery is continuous and feeds a steadily validated backlog into delivery, while delivery's learnings feed back into discovery.

This is why dual-track is best understood as orthogonal to Scrum-vs-Kanban-vs-Shape-Up rather than a competitor to them. You can run dual-track inside Scrum (discovery work alongside the delivery backlog), inside Kanban (a discovery swimlane), or inside Shape Up (the shaping step is discovery). It answers a different question — not "what cadence do we deliver on?" but "how do we keep validating what's worth delivering at all?"

Further reading: jpattonassociates.com — Jeff Patton's article explaining dual-track development — running discovery and delivery in parallel rather than sequentially — so teams continuously learn and validate while shipping; page JS-gated.

Continuous discovery — Teresa Torres and the weekly habit

If dual-track says discovery should run continuously, continuous discovery is the concrete habit that operationalizes it. Teresa Torres's definition, via Learning Loop, is deliberately specific about cadence and ownership: "Weekly touch points with customers, by the team building the product, where they conduct small research activities, in pursuit of a desired product outcome." Three constraints are doing work in that sentence — weekly (not a quarterly research project), by the team building the product (not outsourced to a research function), and in pursuit of an outcome (not generic learning). The aim is to convert discovery from an occasional, heavyweight study into a standing rhythm woven into delivery weeks.

The structuring artifact is the opportunity solution tree: start from a desired outcome at the root, branch into the opportunities (unmet customer needs) that could move it, then the candidate solutions under each opportunity, then the experiments that would test those solutions. Its discipline is traceability — every solution must hang off a real, outcome-linked need rather than appearing as a top-down feature request. This is the structural defense against the most common discovery failure: building a backlog of solutions nobody can connect to a customer problem or a business outcome. The tree makes "why are we building this?" answerable by pointing at a branch.

Torres's method sits inside the same Cagan-lineage product thinking as the empowered product team — the idea that teams should be given problems to solve and outcomes to move, not features to ship. Continuous discovery is the day-to-day practice that makes outcome ownership real; without a standing customer-contact habit, "we own outcomes" degrades into the team guessing. It is widely adopted in principle and unevenly practiced — the weekly cadence is the part teams most often let slip under delivery pressure, which is exactly when the discovery signal matters most.

Further reading: jpattonassociates.com — Jeff Patton's article explaining dual-track development — running discovery and delivery in parallel rather than sequentially — so teams continuously learn and validate while shipping; page JS-gated.

The rituals — what the ceremonies are actually for

The visible surface of most agile processes is a set of recurring meetings, and it helps to read each as a specific feedback loop rather than a calendar obligation. The agile handbook names the four canonical Scrum events: Sprint Planning, where "the Product Owner and Development Team collaborate" to decide what the upcoming sprint will deliver; the Daily Standup, "a brief daily meeting where the Development Team synchronizes their work"; the Sprint Review, where the team demonstrates the increment to stakeholders and gathers feedback; and the Sprint Retrospective, where "the team conducts the Sprint Retrospective to reflect on the previous sprint."

Mapping each ritual to its purpose is what separates a healthy process from cargo-culting. Planning is the commitment-and-alignment loop (what and why). The standup is a coordination loop, not a status loop — its job is to surface blockers and dependencies for the team, not to report progress upward to a manager; when it becomes a round-robin of "yesterday I… today I…" recited to the Scrum Master, it has quietly inverted into the status meeting agile was reacting against. The review is the product feedback loop (did we build something valuable?). The retrospective is the process-improvement loop (how do we work better?) — and it is the one that most directly enacts the "adaptation" pillar, which is why a retro that never changes anything is the surest sign a team is doing mechanical Scrum.

The senior-PM judgment call is ceremony weight. Every ritual is overhead justified only by the loop it closes; a team drowning in meetings is usually running ceremonies whose feedback loop is dead or redundant. The corrective is not to abolish rituals but to ask, per ceremony, "what decision or adjustment does this produce?" — and to cut or merge any that produce none. Kanban's lighter ceremony load and Shape Up's rejection of recurring planning are both, in part, reactions to ritual bloat in poorly-run Scrum.

Estimation — story points, velocity, and the #NoEstimates argument

Estimation is where process debates get most heated, partly because the canonical tool is so easily misused. Mike Cohn's definition: "Story points are a unit of measure for expressing an estimate of the overall effort that will be required to fully implement a product backlog item or any other piece of work" — a single relative number folding volume, complexity, and uncertainty together, deliberately not hours. The discipline is comparative: "What matters are the relative values," so "a user story that is assigned two story points should be twice as much effort as a one-point story." The absolute numbers are arbitrary; only the ratios are meant to carry information. The most common anti-pattern — quietly converting points back into hours, or treating velocity as a productivity target to be maximized — defeats the entire technique and turns a forecasting aid into a surveillance metric.

Whether the whole exercise is worth it is genuinely contested, and the empirical record is unkind to both camps' certainty. Per the research summary, Larry Maccherone's analysis of tens of thousands of teams found "no statistically significant advantage for SP over time-based estimation (or vice versa)" and that "the estimation unit (SP vs. time) has no meaningful impact on forecasting or team effectiveness." That vacuum is the opening the #NoEstimates movement (Vasco Duarte) drives through: rather than argue points versus hours, break work into uniformly small slices, count throughput, and forecast with Monte Carlo simulation on historical actuals — skipping the estimation ceremony altogether.

The honest synthesis, which the same source reaches, is that the estimation unit is largely a red herring: team stability, consistent slicing of work, shared experience, and release frequency are what actually drive predictability. This is a live disagreement — Cohn and the Scrum mainstream still defend points as a useful conversation-forcing tool, while #NoEstimates practitioners view the conversation as waste — and a senior PM is better served knowing the debate is unresolved than picking a side as doctrine. The practical takeaway is that how you estimate matters far less than whether your slices are small and your throughput is measured.

Scaling — the Spotify model and why copying it fails

Coordinating one team is a process problem; coordinating fifty is an organizational-design problem, and the most-copied answer is the "Spotify model." The neutral definition: a squad is "a small, cross-functional team of 6 to 12 people with end-to-end responsibility for a specific feature area, product component, or user journey," nested in tribes (related squads), crossed by chapters (skill-based groups spanning squads) and guilds (voluntary communities of interest). It is the canonical picture of how to keep small-team autonomy while scaling headcount. The critical caveat is in the same source, quoting the 2012 Kniberg/Ivarsson whitepaper that launched it: "It was not a framework to be adopted. It was not a set of practices to follow" — it was a descriptive snapshot of one company at one moment, not a blueprint.

The insider correction is the part every PM considering it should read. Jeremiah Lee, who was at Spotify as it scaled past 3,000 people, reports in "Spotify's Failed Squad Goals" that "the famed squad model was only ever aspirational and never fully implemented," and that the whitepaper was meant to be a series on autonomy, alignment, and accountability of which "only autonomy was documented and promoted." His structural diagnosis is the matrix: "Without a single engineering manager responsible for the engineers on a team, the product manager lacked an equivalent peer," so conflicts escalated awkwardly through multiple chapter leads. His one-line thesis — "autonomy requires alignment" — is precisely what the copycats dropped.

Chameleon's post-mortem generalizes the failure beyond Spotify: a co-creator's admission is the most-quoted line — "Even at the time we wrote it, we weren't doing it. It was part ambition, part approximation" — and the recurring symptom of autonomy without alignment is concrete, an internal coach noting "Every time you have a new team, they have to reinvent the wheel in how they should be working," producing duplicated effort and knowledge silos. The transferable lesson is blunt and applies to importing any named structure: copying the labels (squads, tribes, guilds) without the trust-and-alignment culture that made them function installs the costs without the benefits. The org chart is the easy part to clone and the wrong part to focus on.

Waterfall, stage-gate, and the heavyweight end of the spectrum

It is worth knowing the baseline that agile reacted against, both because it still exists and because it is sometimes the right call. The agile handbook states the contrast plainly: "Unlike traditional 'waterfall' methods, where all development occurs sequentially in one large phase, Agile divides the software development process into smaller iterations or time-boxed cycles." Waterfall runs requirements → design → build → test → release as gated sequential phases, with validation arriving only after the build is largely complete. Its close cousin in physical-product and regulated contexts is stage-gate (phase-gate), where work passes through formal go/kill decision gates between phases. The defining weakness is feedback latency: you discover you built the wrong thing at the end, when changing it is most expensive — which is exactly the failure agile's "respond to change" and "deliver early" principles target.

The senior-PM nuance is that "waterfall = bad" is too crude. Sequential, heavily-specified processes genuinely fit work where requirements are stable and well-understood, where the cost of late change is bounded, or where regulation, safety, or hardware lead-times demand up-front specification and documented gates — aerospace, medical devices, large infrastructure, contractual fixed-scope work. The mistake is using waterfall for genuinely uncertain product work where requirements will change, which is most software. The reciprocal mistake is forcing two-week sprints onto work whose unknowns don't resolve on a two-week clock.

The same heavyweight instinct reappears, in agile clothing, at enterprise scale. SAFe — "The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, is an agile framework developed for development teams" — adds Team, Program, and Portfolio levels and a Release Train Engineer coordinating an Agile Release Train. It answers a real problem honestly stated: "traditional agile methodologies weren't designed to handle multiple interdependent teams," and "when organizations have numerous teams whose work must integrate seamlessly, complete autonomy creates coordination problems." SAFe's critics call it heavy and top-down — re-importing the very ceremony and central planning agile fled — while its defenders argue large interdependent orgs need an explicit alignment layer. It is the structural answer to the same coordination tension the Spotify model tried to answer culturally; which one fits is itself contested and context-dependent.

Choosing and adapting — context over dogma

The thread running through every framework in this primer is that there is no universally correct process — only trade-offs matched (or mismatched) to context. A useful way to choose is to read your situation along the axes the frameworks differ on, rather than starting from a brand. Cadence: does your work arrive in plannable batches (lean Scrum) or as a continuous, unpredictable stream (lean Kanban)? Scope-vs-time: is the deadline the fixed constraint (Shape Up's appetite) or the scope (traditional estimation)? Uncertainty: are requirements stable (sequential/stage-gate is defensible) or genuinely unknown (iterative, with real discovery)? Coordination: one team (pick a delivery framework) or many interdependent teams (a scaling answer, structural like SAFe or cultural like the Spotify ideal)?

The selection signals — complexity, org structure, product-stack size, planning horizon — are a reasonable starting filter, and the cadence/change-tolerance/authority lens sharpens it. But two meta-points matter more than any decision table. First, almost no successful team runs a framework by the book; they adapt it. Scrumban, Scrum-with-continuous-discovery, Shape-Up-principles-without-six-week-cycles — the canon is raw material, not scripture, and the Scrum Guide's own "purposefully incomplete" is permission to wrap your own practices around the skeleton. Second, process is downstream of the things it cannot fix: team skill, psychological safety, clear outcomes, and engineering quality. A good process amplifies a capable team and a clear strategy; it cannot substitute for either.

The failure mode to watch for in yourself and your org is process as identity — "we are a Scrum shop," "we do Shape Up" — which converts a means into a tribe and makes the process resistant to the very inspection-and-adaptation it claims to value. The healthier posture is to treat your process as the current best hypothesis about how this team should work, hold it to the retrospective's scrutiny like any other artifact, and change it when the evidence says to. The teams that ship well are rarely the ones with the purest framework; they are the ones whose operating system actually fits the work in front of them.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

  • Product discovery & validation — the discovery track here is one half of a deeper topic: interview technique, assumption mapping, and experiment design (one hop down from continuous discovery).
  • Prioritization frameworks — RICE, WSJF, and opportunity scoring decide what enters the backlog or the betting table that these processes then execute.
  • Outcomes over outputs & OKRs — the goal-setting layer that gives a process something to optimize for; empowered teams own outcomes, not feature lists.
  • Roadmapping — how the longer-horizon plan reconciles with sprint/cycle-level execution, and why now-next-later roadmaps fit iterative process better than dated Gantt charts.
  • Team topologies & org design — the scaling question pursued rigorously: stream-aligned, platform, enabling, and complicated-subsystem teams as a more durable answer than copying Spotify.
  • Engineering practices (CI/CD, trunk-based development) — the technical-excellence layer the Scrum Guide deliberately omits but the agile principles demand; process without it sprints faster into trouble.

References

  1. jpattonassociates.com ·cited· (T3)

    Jeff Patton's article explaining dual-track development — running discovery and delivery in parallel rather than sequentially — so teams continuously learn and validate while shipping; page JS-gated.

  2. jpattonassociates.com ·cited· (T3)

    Jeff Patton's article explaining dual-track development — running discovery and delivery in parallel rather than sequentially — so teams continuously learn and validate while shipping; page JS-gated.

  3. amazon.com ·cited· (T4)

    Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

  4. amazon.com ·cited· (T4)

    Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

  5. amazon.com ·cited· (T4)

    Amazon listing for Sprint, the book by Jake Knapp and Google Ventures team on the five-day design sprint process for solving big problems and testing ideas quickly.

  6. agilemanifesto.org ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    The 2001 Manifesto for Agile Software Development states its four values as a set of preferences, not absolutes: "Individuals and interactions over processes and tools; Working software over comprehensive documentation; Customer collaboration over contract negotiation; Responding to change over following a plan." The closing line is the part most often forgotten: "That is, while there is value in the items on the right, we value the items on the left more" — the right-hand items are not discarded, they are subordinated. Every process that followed is a different bet on how to honor those four preferences.

  7. agilemanifesto.org ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    The twelve principles behind the Manifesto operationalize the values into working commitments: "Our highest priority is to satisfy the customer through early and continuous delivery of valuable software," "Welcome changing requirements, even late in development," and "Working software is the primary measure of progress." Principle ten — "Simplicity—the art of maximizing the amount of work not done—is essential" — is the one most processes quietly violate. The principles are the testable layer; a team can claim to be agile and still fail most of them.

  8. scrumguides.org ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Schwaber and Sutherland's Scrum Guide defines the framework tersely: "Scrum is a lightweight framework that helps people, teams and organizations generate value through adaptive solutions for complex problems." Two phrases carry the weight. First, "The Scrum framework is purposefully incomplete, only defining the parts required to implement Scrum theory" — Scrum is a skeleton you wrap your own engineering practices around, not a complete process. Second, it rests on three empirical pillars — transparency, inspection, and adaptation — realized through five events (the Sprint container plus Planning, Daily Scrum, Review, Retrospective) and three accountabilities (Product Owner, Scrum Master, Developers), with Sprints fixed at "one month or less."

  9. kanbantool.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Kanban adapts the Toyota production signal to knowledge work through two governing moves: "The Kanban method emphasizes visualizing the workflow and limiting work in progress (WIP)," and "Kanban centers on continuous delivery and encourages teams to optimize their workflow at every opportunity." The mechanism is a pull system — teams draw work as capacity frees up rather than committing a batch upfront — which produces continuous flow and shorter lead times without time-boxed iterations. Where Scrum changes what the team does in discrete sprint-sized steps, Kanban changes the flow continuously and treats WIP limits, not a sprint boundary, as the forcing function.

  10. brightwork.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    BrightWork draws the operational line between the two dominant agile flavors: "Scrum focuses on sprints that are a set period of time whereas Kanban cadence is focused on consistent and continuous workflow," and on stability, "Kanban works best for teams with stable priorities that are unlikely to change over time. Scrum allows for changing priorities from sprint to sprint." The role difference is just as telling — "The Scrum Master acts as the chief problem solver, unlike Kanban where every team member shares responsibility and leadership." Cadence, change-tolerance, and where authority sits are the three axes a team actually chooses on.

  11. medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    AgileInsider reframes the Scrum-vs-Kanban choice as a function of organizational shape rather than taste, sizing it on complexity: "The larger the complexity of deliverables in your organization, the larger its size...you should start veering toward Scrum," while "The smaller the complexity of deliverables, the smaller the size...you should keep hanging onto Kanban." The decision rests on four readable signals — org structure, team structure, the number of products in the stack, and the planning horizon — with quarterly-or-longer horizons and 3+ products pushing toward Scrum's planning ritual and simpler, shorter-horizon work sitting comfortably in Kanban's continuous flow.

  12. basecamp.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Basecamp's Shape Up (Ryan Singer) replaces estimation with appetite — defined in the glossary as "the amount of time we want to spend on a project, as opposed to an estimate." The reframe inverts the question from "how long will this take?" to "how much time is this worth?" so scope flexes to fit a fixed time rather than schedules flexing to fit fixed scope. Work runs in six-week cycles chosen at a betting table during a two-week cooldown, and a circuit breaker cancels anything that fails to ship within its cycle rather than extending it — making the deadline real from day one instead of a renegotiable target.

  13. productplan.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    ProductPlan frames Shape Up as a full alternative grammar to sprint-based work: it gives teams "language and specific techniques to address the risks and unknowns at each stage of product development," organized as three moves — shape, bet, and build — rather than a recurring sprint cadence. The contrast with Scrum is structural, not cosmetic: there is no continuously groomed backlog and no sprint commitment ritual; shaped pitches compete for a bet, and a winning bet gets one fixed cycle of uninterrupted time with variable scope. It is the clearest mainstream counter-proposal to the assumption that agile must mean sprints.

  14. blog.logrocket.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Dual-track agile, per LogRocket, "integrates discovery efforts (determining what to build) with delivery efforts (building) while fostering collaboration across product, engineering, and design." Jeff Patton and Marty Cagan coined "dual-track scrum"/"dual-track agile" around 2012, and the most damaging misreading is structural: the tracks were never meant to be two teams or a sequential handoff — "There are two separate teams for discovery and delivery or separate people with different responsibilities. This was never the intention." The two tracks describe two kinds of work and thinking done by one team in parallel, with discovery continuously feeding a validated backlog into delivery.

  15. learningloop.io ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Continuous discovery is the habit layer that keeps the discovery track honest. Teresa Torres's operational definition is specific about cadence and ownership: "Weekly touch points with customers, by the team building the product, where they conduct small research activities, in pursuit of a desired product outcome." The structuring artifact is the opportunity solution tree — start from a desired outcome, branch into the opportunities (customer needs) that could move it, then the solutions under each, then experiments — which forces solutions to trace back to a real, outcome-linked need rather than a stakeholder request. It reframes research from an occasional project into a standing weekly rhythm.

  16. freecodecamp.org ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    The freeCodeCamp agile handbook names the working rituals plainly — Sprint Planning ("the Product Owner and Development Team collaborate"), the Daily Standup ("a brief daily meeting where the Development Team synchronizes their work"), the Sprint Review (demo to stakeholders for feedback), and the Retrospective ("the team conducts the Sprint Retrospective to reflect on the previous sprint"). It also fixes the baseline these rituals reacted against: "Unlike traditional 'waterfall' methods, where all development occurs sequentially in one large phase, Agile divides the software development process into smaller iterations or time-boxed cycles" — the ceremonies exist to create the feedback loops a single linear phase cannot.

  17. mountaingoatsoftware.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Mike Cohn's canonical framing: "Story points are a unit of measure for expressing an estimate of the overall effort that will be required to fully implement a product backlog item or any other piece of work" — a single relative number folding volume, complexity, and uncertainty together, deliberately not hours. The discipline is comparative, not absolute: "What matters are the relative values," so "a user story that is assigned two story points should be twice as much effort as a one-point story." The absolute numbers are arbitrary; only the ratios are meant to carry information, which is exactly why translating points back into hours quietly defeats the technique.

  18. medium.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    The empirical record undercuts the estimation wars from both sides. Larry Maccherone's analysis of tens of thousands of teams found "no statistically significant advantage for SP over time-based estimation (or vice versa)" and that "the estimation unit (SP vs. time) has no meaningful impact on forecasting or team effectiveness." That vacuum is what the #NoEstimates movement (Vasco Duarte) exploits: break work into uniformly small slices, count throughput, and forecast with Monte Carlo on actuals rather than debating points upfront. The cited consensus is that team stability, consistent slicing, and release frequency drive predictability — not which estimation unit you argue over.

  19. jeremiahlee.com ·cited· (primary-practitioner)

    Jeremiah Lee, who was inside Spotify as it scaled past 3,000 people, is the canonical insider correction: "the famed squad model was only ever aspirational and never fully implemented," and the published whitepaper was meant to be a series on autonomy, alignment, and accountability of which "only autonomy was documented and promoted." The structural failure he names is the matrix: "Without a single engineering manager responsible for the engineers on a team, the product manager lacked an equivalent peer," so conflicts escalated through multiple chapter leads. His thesis — "autonomy requires alignment" — is the line most copycats missed.

  20. peerdom.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Peerdom supplies the neutral definition the critiques argue about: a squad is "a small, cross-functional team of 6 to 12 people with end-to-end responsibility for a specific feature area, product component, or user journey," nested in tribes (related squads), crossed by chapters (skill-based groups) and guilds (voluntary communities of interest). The framing the 2012 Kniberg/Ivarsson whitepaper itself insisted on: "It was not a framework to be adopted. It was not a set of practices to follow" — a descriptive snapshot of one company at one moment. The labels travel easily; the trust-and-autonomy culture underneath them does not.

  21. chameleon.io ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    Chameleon's post-mortem locates the copy-failure in culture, not org chart. A co-creator's admission is the most quoted line: "Even at the time we wrote it, we weren't doing it. It was part ambition, part approximation." The recurring symptom of autonomy without alignment is concrete — an in-house agile coach noting, "Every time you have a new team, they have to reinvent the wheel in how they should be working" — duplicated effort and knowledge silos. The lesson generalizes well past Spotify: importing a named structure without the practices and shared language that made it work installs the costs without the benefits.

  22. productplan.com ·cited· (secondary-curation)

    At the heavyweight end of the scaling spectrum sits SAFe — "The Scaled Agile Framework, or SAFe, is an agile framework developed for development teams" — built on Team, Program, and Portfolio levels with a Release Train Engineer coordinating multiple teams as an Agile Release Train. ProductPlan names the problem it answers honestly: "traditional agile methodologies weren't designed to handle multiple interdependent teams," and "when organizations have numerous teams whose work must integrate seamlessly, complete autonomy creates coordination problems." SAFe trades some autonomy for an explicit alignment-and-oversight layer — the same tension the Spotify model failed to resolve, answered with structure rather than culture.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-15_product-development-processes.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
